content: A causal and stable LTI system with impulse response h(t) produces an output y(t) for an input signal x(t). A signal x(0.5t) is applied to another causal and stable LTI system with impulse response h(0.5t). The resulting output is ____.
explanation:
$$ \text { Given system is shown below, } $$

Output of causal and stable LTI systems

$$ y(t)=x(t) \times h(t) $$

Similarly, for system II

$$ y_1(t)=x(0.5 t) \times h(0.5 t) $$

Using scaling property of convolution

$$ x(a t) \times(a t)=\frac{1}{|a|} y(a t) $$

$$ \begin{aligned} Hence,\,\,\,\,& y_1(t)=\frac{1}{|0.5|} y(0.5 t) \\ & y_1(t)=2 y(0.5 t) \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

content: Consider a four-point moving average filter defined by the equation $$y[n] = \sum\limits_{i = 0}^3 {{\alpha _i}x[n - i]} $$. The condition on the filter coefficients that results in a null at zero frequency is

The direct form structure of an FIR (finite impulse response) filter is shown in the figure.

The filter can be used to approximate a

A system with transfer function H(z) has impulse response h(.) defined as h(2) = 1, h(3) = - 1 and h (k) = 0 otherwise. Consider the following statements.
S1: H(z) is a low-pass filter.
S2: H(z) is an FIR filter.

Which of the following is correct?

options.content: Both S1 and S2 are false.
Both S1 and S2 are true, and S2 is a reason for S1.
Both S1 and S2 are true, but  S2 is not a reason for S1.

content: A signal x(n)$$ = \sin ({\omega _0}\,n + \phi )$$ is the input to a linear time-invariant system having a frequency response $$H({e^{j\omega }})$$.If the output of the system is $$Ax(n - {n_0})$$, then the most general form of $$\angle H({e^{j\omega }})$$ will be

options.content: $$ - {n_0}{\omega _0} + 2\pi k$$ for any arbitrary integer k
$${n_0}{\omega _0} + 2\pi k$$ for any arbitrary integer k

content: A signal x(t) = $$\exp ( - 2\pi Bt)\,u(t)$$ is the input to an ideal low pass filter with bandwidth B Hz. The output is denoted by y(t). Evaluate $$\int\limits_{ - \infty }^\infty  {{{[y(t) - x(t)]}^{2\,}}dt} $$.
